required.title: [Book]
required.author: Roger Priddy or *Cars, Trucks, and Things That Go* by Richard Scarry

required.title: [Tools] Shop rags, flashlight, funnel, basic wrench set

suggested.title: [Video] YouTube: "How a Car Engine Works — Animagraffs"
[Activity] Visit a mechanic's shop, a car show, or a junkyard

supplementary.title: [Model] A small engine model kit (Haynes or similar)

competence_markers.description: The student can identify and name major under-hood components: engine, battery, radiator, air filter, coolant reservoir, brake fluid reservoir, dipstick, and serpentine belt
The student can check engine oil level using the dipstick and report whether it is at the correct level
The student can name the five essential vehicle fluids (oil, coolant, brake fluid, transmission fluid, fuel) and describe what each does
The student demonstrates automatic safety behavior around vehicles: no reaching into running engines, no enclosed-space engine operation, awareness of hot surfaces
The student can describe the basic flow of how a vehicle works: fuel in, combustion, motion out, exhaust out, fluids keep everything working
The student can use their senses to observe a running vehicle and identify normal versus abnormal conditions

content.why_this_matters:
A car is not a magic box. It is a machine — a collection of systems that work together to convert fuel into motion. Every part has a purpose. Every system follows rules. And when you understand those rules, the machine is no longer mysterious. It is comprehensible.

Right now, when your family gets in the car and turns the key (or pushes the start button), the engine starts, the car moves, and you arrive where you are going. It seems simple and automatic. But beneath the hood, hundreds of parts are working in coordination: fuel is being mixed with air and ignited, pistons are moving up and down thousands of times per minute, fluids are circulating to keep everything cool and lubricated, electrical systems are monitoring and controlling everything, and all of this is translating into the wheels turning on the road.

You do not need to understand all of that right now. But you can begin. In this packet, you will learn to identify the major parts of a vehicle, understand what fluids keep a machine running and why, develop safety awareness around machines, and begin to see the car in your driveway not as a mysterious appliance but as a system you can learn to understand — and eventually maintain, diagnose, and repair.

This is the beginning of mechanical independence. The person who understands machines is not helpless when something goes wrong. They are not intimidated by a check engine light, not captive to whatever a mechanic tells them, not stranded on the side of the road without any idea what happened. Understanding machines is understanding freedom.
content.what_this_develops:
1. **Vehicle component identification** — the student can name and locate major external and under-hood components
2. **Fluid awareness** — understanding that machines require fluids to function and what each fluid does
3. **The concept of a system** — seeing a vehicle as an interconnected collection of systems, not a single object
4. **Safety around machines** — automatic respect for the dangers of running engines, heavy vehicles, and exhaust
5. **Basic flow understanding** — fuel goes in, engine converts it to motion, exhaust comes out, fluids keep everything working
6. **Curiosity without fear** — the habit of looking at a machine and asking "how does this work?" rather than "I do not understand this"
content.core_content:
### Part 1: A Vehicle Is Not One Thing — It Is Many Systems Working Together

When you look at a car, you see one object. But a car is actually many systems, each doing a different job, all working together. It is like your body — you are one person, but you have a circulatory system (blood), a respiratory system (breathing), a skeletal system (bones), a nervous system (brain and nerves), and more. Each system does its job, and together they keep you alive and moving.

A vehicle has systems too:

- **Engine** — converts fuel into motion. This is the heart of the vehicle.
- **Fuel system** — stores and delivers fuel to the engine. Like your digestive system — it processes what the engine needs to run.
- **Cooling system** — keeps the engine from overheating. Like sweating — it removes excess heat.
- **Lubrication system** — oil that keeps moving parts from grinding against each other. Like the fluid in your joints.
- **Electrical system** — battery, alternator, and wires that power the starter, lights, ignition, and controls. Like your nervous system.
- **Exhaust system** — carries burned gases away from the engine and out the tailpipe. Like breathing out.
- **Braking system** — slows and stops the vehicle. The most important safety system.
- **Drivetrain** — transmits the engine's power to the wheels. Like your legs — they transfer the energy your body produces into movement.

Each system can be understood. Each system follows rules. And when something goes wrong with the vehicle, it is because one of these systems has a problem — not because the car is "broken" in some vague, unknowable way.

### Part 2: Under the Hood — What You Can See and Name

Open the hood of your vehicle with your mentor. You are going to identify and name the major components.

**The engine:** The large metal block in the center. This is where fuel and air are mixed, compressed, and ignited to produce the power that moves the vehicle. It is heavy, it gets very hot when running, and it has many parts attached to it.

**The battery:** A rectangular box, usually near one corner of the engine bay. It stores electrical energy and provides the power to start the engine. It has two terminals on top: positive (+, usually red) and negative (-, usually black). The battery is connected to the vehicle's electrical system by thick cables.

**The radiator:** A wide, thin component at the front of the engine bay, behind the grille. The radiator cools the engine coolant — hot coolant flows from the engine through the radiator, where air passing through the fins removes the heat, and the cooled fluid returns to the engine.

**The air filter housing:** A box or canister connected to the engine by a large tube or duct. Air enters through this housing, passes through a filter that removes dust and debris, and enters the engine where it mixes with fuel.

**Fluid reservoirs:** Several containers (usually translucent plastic) hold fluids:
- *Coolant reservoir:* Contains engine coolant (usually green, orange, or pink). Marked with "min" and "max" lines.
- *Windshield washer fluid:* Contains fluid for cleaning the windshield. Usually has a windshield symbol on the cap.
- *Brake fluid reservoir:* Contains hydraulic fluid for the braking system. Usually on top of the master cylinder near the back of the engine bay.
- *Power steering fluid reservoir:* Contains fluid for the power steering system (not all vehicles have this — electric power steering does not use fluid).

**The dipstick:** A metal rod with a handle (usually yellow or orange) that slides into the engine. Pull it out, wipe it clean, push it back in, and pull it out again to check the engine oil level. The oil should be between the "low" and "full" marks.

**Belts:** Rubber belts (usually a single serpentine belt) that wrap around pulleys on the front of the engine. The belt transfers the engine's rotational power to the alternator (which charges the battery), the air conditioning compressor, and the power steering pump.

### Part 3: Fluids — The Lifeblood of Machines

Machines need fluids the same way your body needs blood. Each fluid has a specific job, and if any fluid gets too low, too dirty, or disappears completely, the machine suffers — sometimes catastrophically.

**Engine oil:** Lubricates the moving parts inside the engine. Without oil, metal parts grind against each other, generate enormous heat, and destroy the engine in minutes. Engine oil is typically amber when new and darkens to brown or black as it collects contaminants. Checking the oil level with the dipstick is the simplest and most important maintenance check.

**Coolant (antifreeze):** Carries heat away from the engine. The engine produces enormous heat during combustion — enough to melt its own components if not cooled. Coolant circulates from the engine through the radiator and back, continuously removing heat. If the coolant level drops too low, the engine overheats, which can warp metal components and destroy the engine.

**Brake fluid:** Transmits the force of your foot on the brake pedal to the brake pads at the wheels through hydraulic pressure. Brake fluid must be at the correct level and free of air bubbles. If brake fluid is low or contaminated, the brakes may not work — and a vehicle that cannot stop is the most dangerous machine on the road.

**Transmission fluid:** Lubricates and cools the transmission (the system that translates engine speed into wheel speed). Low or degraded transmission fluid causes rough shifting, overheating, and eventually transmission failure — one of the most expensive vehicle repairs.

**Fuel:** Gasoline or diesel. The energy source. Without fuel, the engine does not run. The fuel gauge on the dashboard tells you how much fuel is in the tank. Running completely out of fuel can damage the fuel pump and is entirely preventable.

### Part 4: Safety Around Machines

Vehicles and engines are among the most dangerous things you will be around in your daily life. The safety rules here are non-negotiable.

**A running engine has moving parts that can crush and cut.** The belts, pulleys, and fan spin at high speed. Fingers, loose clothing, long hair, and jewelry can be caught and pulled in. Never reach into the engine bay of a running vehicle. Never wear loose clothing or dangling jewelry near a running engine. Tie back long hair.

**Exhaust contains invisible poison.** A running engine produces carbon monoxide — a colorless, odorless gas that kills by replacing oxygen in your blood. You cannot smell it. You cannot see it. Never run an engine in a closed garage or enclosed space. Even in an open garage, ensure ventilation. Carbon monoxide poisoning can cause unconsciousness and death in minutes.

**Vehicles are heavy and can roll.** A car weighs between 3,000 and 5,000 pounds. If it rolls, it will crush whatever — or whoever — is in its path. Always set the parking brake when a vehicle is parked. Never get under a vehicle that is supported only by a jack — use jack stands (solid metal supports) that can hold the weight if the jack fails.

**Hot surfaces.** An engine that has been running is extremely hot — coolant temperature is typically around 200 degrees Fahrenheit, and exhaust components can exceed 500 degrees. Do not touch engine components, exhaust pipes, or radiator hoses until the engine has cooled. If you need to check fluid levels, wait at least 30 minutes after the engine is turned off.

**Fuel is flammable.** Gasoline vapor is explosive. Never smoke near fuel. Never expose fuel to open flame or sparks. If you smell gasoline strongly near a vehicle, do not start the engine until the cause is identified.

### Part 5: The Basic Flow — How a Vehicle Works

At the simplest level, a vehicle works like this:

1. **Fuel** enters the engine from the fuel tank through the fuel system
2. **Air** enters through the air filter and mixes with the fuel
3. The fuel-air mixture is **compressed** (squeezed) in the engine cylinders
4. A spark plug **ignites** the mixture, which explodes and pushes a piston down
5. The piston's movement is converted to **rotation** by the crankshaft
6. The rotation passes through the **transmission** (which selects the right gear ratio) to the **wheels**
7. The wheels turn and the vehicle **moves**
8. The burned gases exit through the **exhaust system**
9. Meanwhile, **oil** keeps the moving parts lubricated, **coolant** keeps the engine from overheating, and the **electrical system** keeps the spark plugs firing and the instruments working

This is the four-stroke cycle: intake (fuel and air enter), compression (the mixture is squeezed), power (ignition pushes the piston), exhaust (burned gases leave). Every gasoline engine in every car on the road runs on this cycle, thousands of times per minute. You will learn it in much greater detail in Weathering Storms — but the basic flow is the foundation.

### Part 6: Looking at Machines with Understanding

From this point forward, you can look at any vehicle — your family car, a truck on the highway, a tractor in a field — and see it differently. It is not a mysterious machine. It is a collection of systems, each following rules you are beginning to understand.

When the car starts on a cold morning, you know: the battery provided electricity to the starter motor, which turned the engine. Fuel and air entered the cylinders. The spark plugs ignited the mixture. The engine started running. Oil is circulating. Coolant is warming up. The alternator is charging the battery. All of this happened in the seconds between turning the key and hearing the engine run.

When you see a car on the side of the road with steam coming from under the hood, you know: the cooling system failed. The engine overheated. The coolant may have leaked out or the radiator fan may have stopped working. The driver pulled over because continuing to drive with an overheating engine would destroy it.

This is what understanding gives you: the ability to see what is happening instead of just seeing that something happened. This understanding will grow as you progress through this sub-domain — but it starts here, with the ability to name the parts, understand the fluids, and see the flow.

mentor_guide.competence_evaluation:
### 1. Component Identification
**Developing well:** The student can open the hood (with assistance for the prop rod) and point to and name the major components: engine, battery, radiator, air filter housing, coolant reservoir, dipstick, and serpentine belt. They can describe what each component does in their own words — not memorized definitions, but genuine understanding.

**Developing slowly:** The student can identify one or two components but confuses others or cannot describe their function.

**Diagnostic questions:** Has the student had enough under-hood time? One session is not sufficient. Return to the engine bay multiple times over weeks, each time reviewing what they know and adding new detail. Has the student handled the dipstick and looked at the fluid reservoirs, or only pointed at them?

### 2. Fluid Awareness
**Developing well:** The student can name the five essential fluids, describe what each does, and demonstrate checking at least the oil level. They understand that low or absent fluids cause machine damage or failure.

**Developing slowly:** The student confuses fluids or cannot explain why they matter.

**Diagnostic questions:** Have you connected fluid checks to consequences? "If the oil runs out, the engine parts grind together and destroy the engine — a repair that costs thousands of dollars. Checking the oil takes 30 seconds." Real consequences make fluids memorable.

### 3. Safety Behavior
**Developing well:** The student automatically keeps hands clear of belts and pulleys, does not lean into the engine bay with loose clothing, stays away from hot surfaces, and never approaches a running engine without mentor supervision. This behavior is consistent and unprompted.

**Developing slowly:** The student needs frequent safety reminders or is careless about approaching the engine bay.

**Diagnostic questions:** Have you explained the specific injury mechanisms in age-appropriate terms? "The belt spins so fast you cannot see it moving. If your finger touches it, it will pull your hand in before you can react." Concrete, specific consequences are more effective than vague warnings.

### 4. Basic Flow Understanding
**Developing well:** The student can describe the basic flow of vehicle operation: fuel comes in, mixes with air, is ignited, pistons move, wheels turn, exhaust goes out, and fluids keep everything working. They understand that a vehicle is multiple systems working together.

**Developing slowly:** The student sees the vehicle as a single mysterious thing rather than a collection of understandable systems.

**Diagnostic questions:** Have you used the body analogy (circulatory system = cooling system, digestive system = fuel system, nervous system = electrical system)? Have you had the student draw the flow diagram? Drawing forces organization of understanding.
